text: Welcome to another deep dive. Today, we have a very special mission. We are looking directly at
one single ancient text, just one chapter. Right. We are focusing entirely on Seppi Samuel chapter
three. Yeah. And it is an amazing story. It really is. It's about a broken nation,
dangerous politics, and very deep human emotions. We are spending our whole time today inside just
this one chapter. Because there's just so much happening in these few verses. I mean,
you have ambition, murder, quiet tears, loud silences. Exactly. And to really understand
what is happening under the surface, we're going to use three special tools today. Three lenses.
Yes. Three great ways to look at the story. First, we will use the Seventh-day Adventist
Bible commentary from Logos. This gives us the hard facts, the cultural and historical background.
It tells us how people lived back then, what their rules were. Right. Second, we will look
at the writings of Ellen G. White. We're going to use her spiritual analysis to
really look at the characters' hearts. She is very good at showing us the long-term results of bad
choices. And third, we are using a book by Robert Alter called The Art of Biblical Narrative.
He looks at this as a piece of literature. He shows us how the writer uses clever storytelling
tricks, things like dialogue or when people do not speak at all. Yes. The silences. It is
fascinating. So let's set the stage for you, the listener. Where are we when chapter three begins?
Okay. So the situation is very tense.
Israel is split in half. It is basically a civil war. A divided country. Right. In the south,
David is the king. He is ruling from a city called Hebron, but he only controls one tribe,
the tribe of Judah. Just one tribe. Yes. And up in the north, there is a different king.
His name is Ish-bosheth. He is the son of the old king, King Saul. So Ish-bosheth wears the crown
in the north. He wears the crown, yes. But he does not really hold the power. The real power
in the north belongs to his army commander, a man named Abner. Abner.
He is the guy pulling all the strings behind the curtain. Exactly. Abner is the real boss.
So we have two kings, one broken country, and a very powerful general.
That leads us right into the first verse of chapter three. The text says there was a long
war between the house of Saul and the house of David. A long war. That word is so important.
The Adventist Bible commentary points this out. This was not just one quick battle.
It was a slow, painful war. A war of attrition.
Defeating the country day by day. Farms are ruined. Families are fighting each other. It is
just a slow bleed for the whole society. But the Bible tells us exactly who was
winning this slow war. It says David grew stronger and stronger, and the house of Saul
grew weaker and weaker. Power is slowly shifting south. Yeah. And Ellen White has a really
interesting point about this. She looks at how David is acting during this time. Right. Because
David is actually being very patient. He is. He knows God promised him the whole kingdom,
but he is not forcing it. He is not forcing it. He is not forcing it. He is not forcing it. He is not
launching huge, angry attacks to kill Saul's family. He is waiting for God to do it in a
natural way. But Abner, the general in the north, he's the exact opposite. White points out that
Abner is the one pushing for aggressive war. Abner sees his side getting weaker, so he fights
harder. He wants to keep his personal power. He is trying to force history to go his way with a
sword. It is a huge difference in leadership. Trusting time versus forcing control. It really
is. But then...
Right after talking about this terrible war, the text does something very interesting.
It changes focus completely. Yes. It takes us inside the city walls of Hebron,
right into David's private house. Verses two through five, we get a list,
a very specific list of David's family. It says David has six sons born to six different women,
six wives. And the text names them all. Amnon, Chuliab, Absalom, Adonijah, Shephatiah,
and Ithrim. Right in the middle of a war story, we get a list of wives,
and babies. Why? I mean, why put that here? Well, the Adventist commentary helps us understand
the culture. Back in the ancient Middle East, this is what successful kings did.
Having many wives was a political move. Exactly. Polygamy was tolerated in that culture.
It showed everyone that the king was rich and powerful, and it helped him make alliances with
other important families. So David is just doing what all the other kings do. He is building a
normal royal court for that time. He is following the culture. But the commentary makes a very clear
point.
Just because the culture tolerates it does not mean it was God's original plan.
God's plan for marriage did not include multiple wives.
No, it did not. And by making this choice, David is actually planting seeds that will
grow into terrible problems later. This is where Ellen White's analysis is so sharp.
She says the deep hatred, the jealousy, the violence that will ruin David's family later,
it all starts right here with this list.
Think about those names. Amnon is the firstborn, later in the story. He does something horrible,
to his own sister.
And Absalom. He eventually starts a massive rebellion. He tries to kill David and steal
the throne.
Right. And White says we can trace those moral failures in the children straight back to the
father's choices.
David compromised. He broke a core family rule to build his political power.
And he created a house full of competing mothers and angry sons.
It looks like success on the outside, but it is a dark shadow.
And this really means something for you, the listener. It makes you think about your own
choices.
Small compromise.
Exactly. David probably thought he was just doing what he had to do, just do a normal business.
He didn't see the pain he was bringing to his kids years later.
It shows that choices that seem totally fine in our current culture
might actually bring a lot of destruction into our homes later on.
Actions have echoes. And sometimes we don't hear the echo until years later.
That is a very serious thought.
